title: Multi-Query Attention
description: An attention variant that shares one key-value head across all query heads to minimize key-value cache memory.
openingSummary: Multi-query attention (MQA) is an attention variant that keeps multiple query heads but shares one key-value pair across them, shrinking the key-value cache for long-context inference.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Multi-query attention (MQA) is an attention variant derived from multi-head attention. Every query head still computes its own attention scores, but all heads read from the same key head and value head.

whyItExists.title: Why It Exists
whyItExists.body: MQA targets key-value cache size and memory bandwidth during autoregressive decoding. Sharing one key-value pair across all query heads minimizes the number of tensors stored per token.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: Linear projections still produce multiple query heads, but the key and value projections collapse to a single head each. Every query head attends against the shared key and value tensors, and the outputs are then concatenated and projected as usual.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: The formulas below contrast how multi-head attention pairs every query head with its own key-value heads versus multi-query attention, which routes all query heads through one shared key-value pair.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: Compared with multi-head attention, MQA stores one key-value pair instead of one pair per head. Compared with grouped-query attention, MQA compresses further by sharing a single key-value head across all query heads rather than several groups.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: MQA appears in decoder-only language models that prioritize inference throughput and lean key-value caches, including some large-scale serving configurations that accept the representational tradeoff.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: Sharing one key-value head across all queries can reduce representational flexibility versus full multi-head attention or grouped-query attention. Quality-sensitive workloads may prefer grouped-query attention or multi-head attention.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: MQA established the head-sharing design space. It shows how far key-value compression can go before grouped-query attention offers a middle ground between cache size and query diversity.

mqa.queryHeadFlexibility: H query heads share one key-value pair

mha.kvHeadCount: H key heads and H value heads
mha.queryHeadFlexibility: H independent query and key-value head pairs

gqa.kvHeadCount: G key heads and G value heads
gqa.queryHeadFlexibility: H distinct query heads grouped into G shared key-value pairs

k.definition: Shared key vectors from one key-value head.

v.definition: Shared value vectors from one key-value head.

mhaKvHeads.label: H key-value head pairs (1:1 with queries)

mhaOneToOne.label: Each query head has its own K and V

mqaSharedMapping.label: All H query heads read from one K and V

mqaSharingNote.label: One shared key/value pair serves every query head
